source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Many moons are in circular orbit about a planet. The angular velocity of a moon is $\omega$ when the orbit of the moon has a radius $r$ about the planet. Fig. 1.2 shows the variation of $r^{3}$ with $1 / \omega^{2}$ for these moons. $r^{3}$. Using $G$ as the gravitational constant, determine an expression for the mass $M$ of the planet.

metadata.reasoning_chain: $\frac{GMm}{r^{2}}=mr\omega^{2}$\n\n$$ M=\frac{r^{3}\omega^{2}}{G} \text{ and gradient }=r^{3}\omega^{2} \text{ hence } M=\frac{\text{ gradient }}{G} \quad \text{ or } \quad r^{3}=GM\times \frac{1}{\omega^{2}} \text{ so gradient }=GM \text{ hence } M=\frac{\text{ gradient }}{G}$$
